description: The provided image pixels contain only a CrossMark publication integrity logo with no discernible scientific figure content; therefore, all substantive information is inferred from the accompanying text extraction summary. The paper concerns Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 700–1200 °C for 1 h after hot rolling. The microstructural evolution involves α-ferrite, γ-austenite, and κ-carbide. At 700 °C, γ fully decomposes into lamellar α + κ eutectoid with continuous κ along boundaries. Between 800 °C and 850 °C, κ coarsens and partially dissolves. Above 900 °C, matrix κ disappears, yielding an α + γ duplex structure whose grains grow markedly with temperature (α₁ from ~3.5 μm to ~22 μm, γ from ~5.0 μm to ~36 μm). Tensile data show peak strength–ductility synergy at 900 °C (UTS 855 MPa, YS 651 MPa, elongation 36.6 %). The core conclusion is that κ-carbide dissolution and grain growth govern the trade-off between strength and ductility, with intermediate temperatures offering optimal performance.

This figure presents a temperature-time schematic illustrating the thermomechanical processing and subsequent annealing schedule for the investigated Fe-10Mn-10Al-0.7C low-density steel. The process route comprises two stages. First, the material is homogenized at 1200 °C for 1 h, hot-rolled with a finishing temperature around 880 °C, rapidly water-cooled to approximately 300 °C, and then air-cooled. Second, the hot-rolled plate is reheated to various annealing temperatures between 700 °C and 1200 °C (represented by multiple horizontal hold lines), held for 1 h, and finally water-quenched. Key labeled parameters include the 1200 °C homogenization, the 880 °C finish-rolling temperature, the 300 °C intermediate quench temperature, and the 700-1200 °C annealing window with isochronal 1 h holds. The caption states these are the hot-rolling and annealing conditions. This thermal history map defines the baseline processing route used to systematically investigate how annealing temperature affects microstructural evolution, phase fractions, and tensile properties across the studied specimens.

This figure presents the equilibrium phase-fraction versus temperature curves for the Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel calculated by Thermo-Calc with the TCFE7 database. The x-axis spans approximately 500–1500 °C and the y-axis shows phase fraction from 0 to 1.0. Four distinct curves/regions are visible: ferrite (α, BCC), austenite (γ, FCC), κ-carbide (κ), and liquid (L). The α fraction decreases with falling temperature until roughly 1000 °C, then increases again; the γ phase dominates the intermediate temperature range, forming a closed loop that peaks near 0.65–0.70. The caption and citation text state that κ-carbide appears below about 823 °C via the eutectoid reaction γ → α + κ. A small amount of δ-ferrite is predicted to persist without fully transforming to austenite. The liquid phase emerges only above ~1350 °C. The plot provides the thermodynamic baseline for interpreting microstructural evolution during annealing (700–1200 °C), although the authors note that the actual measured dissolution temperature of κ-carbide (850–900 °C) is higher than the calculated equilibrium value, attributing the discrepancy to non-equilibrium conditions and the difference between atomic fractions (calculation) and volume fractions (XRD measurement).

This figure presents the microstructure of the as-hot-rolled Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimen (marked “HR”) using a combination of optical and scanning electron microscopy across different section planes. Subfigure (a) is an OM micrograph of the RD–ND plane (rolling direction–normal direction) with a 50 μm scale bar, revealing a typical banded structure comprising alternating elongated α-ferrite and γ-austenite layers, where coarse ferrite grains formed during the early stage of solidification are clearly visible. Subfigure (b) is an OM micrograph of the RD–TD plane (rolling direction–transverse direction) at the same magnification, showing a highly inhomogeneous microstructure consisting of large continuous α-phase areas and γ-phase areas, as defined in the caption. Subfigure (c) is a higher-magnification SEM micrograph of the RD–TD plane with a 2 μm scale bar, resolving fine κ-carbide particles precipitated along ferrite/austenite (α/γ) and ferrite/ferrite (α/α) boundaries. The caption reports that EDS confirms the α phase is Al-rich, the γ phase is Mn-rich and C-rich, and the κ-carbide is Mn-rich and Al-rich. Collectively, these images establish that the hot-rolled baseline microstructure is a heterogeneous duplex α+γ matrix decorated with interphase κ-carbide.

Figure 4 presents stacked X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel in the hot-rolled (HR) condition and after annealing at 700–1200 °C for 1 h. The x-axis spans 2θ from approximately 30° to 90° and the y-axis shows intensity in counts per second. Nine offset curves are displayed with temperature labels from HR to 1200 °C, and phase labels (γ, α, κ-carbide) are marked near the 800 °C trace. The pattern for the HR specimen shows multiple diffraction peaks, and the caption states that κ-carbide peaks are not detected, probably due to low volume fraction or coherency with the α matrix. In the 700 °C annealed specimen, additional peaks attributed to κ-carbide appear, and the caption reports that no obvious γ phase peaks are seen. At 800 °C and 850 °C, peaks for all three phases are visible. As the annealing temperature reaches 900 °C and above, the κ-carbide peaks vanish, leaving only α and γ reflections. The caption reports calculated lattice parameters of 2.89–2.91 Å for α, 3.65–3.67 Å for γ, and 3.76–3.79 Å for κ-carbide, indicating lattice expansion caused by Mn, Al, and C. These results demonstrate that κ-carbide dissolution occurs between 850 °C and 900 °C, confirming the strong temperature dependence of phase stability in this steel.

Figure 5 presents optical micrographs of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at 800 °C, 850 °C, and 900 °C for 1 h. Subfigures (a), (c), and (e) display the RD–ND section microstructures, while (b), (d), and (f) show the RD–TD sections. At 800 °C, a banded structure with long strip α-ferrite remains, and κ-carbide is visible mainly between α and γ phases or along α/α boundaries, with some dispersion in γ areas. At 850 °C, κ-carbide within γ regions disappears and reduces near γ areas, yet continuous κ-carbide along α/α boundaries persists. At 900 °C, no obvious κ-carbide is observed, indicating nearly complete dissolution into the α and γ matrices. The figure demonstrates that increasing annealing temperature promotes κ-carbide dissolution and alters phase distribution, with the actual dissolution temperature lying between 850 °C and 900 °C.

This figure presents a series of SEM micrographs illustrating the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at temperatures from 700 °C to 1200 °C for 1 h. Subfigures (a) through (g) display the RD–TD section, while (h) shows the RD–ND section. At 700 °C (a), the γ phase has almost completely decomposed into a lamellar eutectoid mixture of α-ferrite and κ-carbide, with continuous κ-carbide decorating α grain boundaries. At 800 °C (b) and 850 °C (c), the γ fraction increases while κ-carbide coarsens toward a globular morphology and its overall fraction decreases. Between 900 °C (d) and 1100 °C (g), no obvious κ-carbide is visible in the matrix; only trace boundary κ-carbide forms along some α/α grain boundaries during quenching, accompanied by significant grain growth of both α and γ phases. At 1200 °C (h), the microstructure consists of very large equiaxed α and γ grains, with caption-reported needle-like or plate-like FCC particles inside some large α grains. The progressive dissolution of κ-carbide between 850 and 900 °C and the consequent coarsening of the duplex α+γ microstructure above 900 °C are central to understanding the loss of precipitation strengthening and the resulting tensile property trends.

This figure presents TEM micrographs and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) characterization of a Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimen annealed at 700 °C for 1 h. Panel (a) is a bright-field (BF) image with a 500 nm scale bar, revealing alternating lamellae of α-ferrite (α) and κ-carbide (κ) produced by the eutectoid reaction γ → α + κ. Panel (b) shows the SAED pattern and its schematic index from [110]κ, with visible labels indicating [001]α // [011]κ and (110)α // (1-11)κ; caption states this confirms the Nishiyama–Wasserman orientation relationship between lamellar κ-carbide and the α matrix. Panel (c) is a (200)κ dark-field (DF) image with a 200 nm scale bar, highlighting the spatial distribution of the κ-carbide lamellae within the α matrix. Panel (d) is a BF image with a 200 nm scale bar showing globular or short-rod-like κ particles dispersed in the α matrix, which caption reports show no orientation relationship with the α phase. The overall evidence supports that the eutectoid lamellar κ maintains crystallographic coherency with α, whereas coherency and orientation relationships are destroyed during κ-carbide coarsening, yielding incoherent globular morphologies.

Figure 8 presents combined transmission electron microscopy analysis of the Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimen annealed at 800 °C for 1 h. Panel (a) is a bright-field TEM micrograph with a 500 nm scale bar, showing the coexistence of ferrite (α), austenite (γ), and κ-carbide (κ) particles, where the κ phase appears as globular or short-rod-like precipitates distributed among the α and γ grains. Panel (b) displays selected-area diffraction patterns (SADPs) acquired from the corresponding α, κ, and γ regions in (a), with zone axes identified as [111] for α, [001] for κ, and [011] for γ, together with indexed reflections such as {110}α, {100}κ, and {200}γ. The accompanying text reports that, unlike the lamellar eutectoid structure formed at 700 °C which exhibits a Nishiyama–Wasserman orientation relationship, no orientation relationship is found between the κ-carbide and neighboring α phase after annealing at 800 °C, indicating that coherency is destroyed during coarsening. This figure thus demonstrates that 800 °C annealing produces coarsened, incoherent κ-carbide with reduced volume fraction and globular morphology, representing a critical microstructural transition in the annealing sequence.

This figure presents a line plot (Fig. 9) showing the average grain size evolution of individual phases in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel as a function of annealing temperature from 900 °C to 1200 °C, with data obtained from SEM micrographs and error bars representing standard deviations. Three distinct curves are plotted: α₂ (banded shape, pink diamonds) representing large, continuously distributed banded ferrite grains that solidified first during casting; γ (blue triangles) representing austenite; and α₁ (red squares) representing ferrite mainly distributed among γ grains. The α₂ phase exhibits the largest grain size, increasing from approximately 26 μm at 900 °C to about 68 μm at 1200 °C, while γ increases from roughly 5 μm to 36 μm over the same range. The α₁ phase shows the smallest grain size, initially decreasing slightly from 900 °C to 950 °C before rising to approximately 22 μm at 1200 °C. The caption states that the two ferrite types are treated separately due to their large difference in average grain size. The rapid grain growth of α₂ and γ above 900 °C, contrasted with the sluggish growth of α₁ until above 1000 °C, supports the conclusion that grain coarsening of these phases is the dominant microstructural factor controlling tensile property degradation at higher annealing temperatures, as interpreted via the Hall–Petch relationship.

This figure presents four scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs labeled (a) through (d), showing boundary regions in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at 900 °C, 1000 °C, 1100 °C and 1200 °C for 1 h, respectively. Subfigures (a)–(c) reveal that κ-carbide precipitates persist along α/α grain boundaries (annotated as "boundary κ") even when the annealing temperature exceeds the dissolution temperature of matrix κ-carbide, with the grain structure coarsening markedly as temperature increases from 900 °C to 1100 °C. Subfigure (d), corresponding to 1200 °C, shows not only boundary κ-carbide but also numerous needle-like or plate-like FCC particles within large α grains, which the caption states form during quenching via rapid BCC-to-FCC transformation and compete with boundary κ-carbide for Mn and C. The scale bars are 10 μm for (a) and (b), 5 μm for (c), and 15 μm for (d). Together, these images support the conclusion that boundary κ-carbide forms during quenching when the annealing temperature is above the dissolution temperature, and that extreme annealing temperatures promote additional intragranular FCC precipitation alongside grain-boundary carbides.

This figure presents TEM micrographs of boundary κ-carbide in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 1100 °C for 1 h. Panel (a) is a bright-field (BF) micrograph showing a thin κ phase (indicated by an arrow and labeled κ) situated along a boundary between α-ferrite grains (labeled α), with a scale bar of 500 nm. Panel (b) displays selected-area diffraction patterns (SADPs) acquired from the α matrix along the [001] zone axis and from the κ-carbide along the [1-12] zone axis, with visible indexed spots including (-110), (110), (200), (111), and (-111). The SADPs confirm the crystal structure and orientation of the boundary phase. The caption states that the actual dissolution temperature for κ-carbide lies between 850 °C and 900 °C, which is higher than the equilibrium Thermo-Calc value of about 823 °C, suggesting disequilibrium effects. The citation notes that such boundary κ-carbide is found along large α/α boundaries in the 900–1200 °C annealing range and is believed to form during quenching after annealing above the dissolution temperature.

Figure 12 presents transmission electron microscopy analysis of boundary κ-carbide in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 1200 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching. Panel (a) is a bright-field (BF) micrograph showing a thin κ-carbide precipitate (labeled κ) along an α/α grain boundary within the α matrix (labeled α), with a scale bar of 200 nm. Panel (b) shows the selected-area diffraction pattern (SADP) of α and κ together with an indexed schematic illustrating the Nishiyama–Wasserman (N–W) orientation relationship: [001]α ∥ [011]κ and (110)α ∥ (11̄1)κ. Panel (c) is a (200)κ dark-field (DF) micrograph imaged along the [110]κ direction, revealing that only portions of the boundary κ-carbide are illuminated. As stated in the caption and text, the partial contrast in the DF image indicates large misorientation between neighboring κ-carbide particles. These observations confirm that boundary κ-carbide forms during quenching when the annealing temperature exceeds the κ-carbide dissolution temperature (~850–900 °C), exhibiting an N–W relationship with the α matrix but significant orientation variation along the boundary.

This figure presents SEM EDS line-scanning results for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at (a) 800 °C and (b) 900 °C for 1 h. Each subpanel pairs a backscattered-electron SEM micrograph (top) with the corresponding compositional profiles (bottom) for Al K (red solid), Mn K (blue dashed), and Fe K (black dotted) versus position. In the 800 °C specimen, distinct κ-carbide particles are visible along and near α/γ boundaries, and the EDS profiles reveal pronounced Mn and Al enrichment in κ-carbide relative to both matrix phases; caption states that α phase contains higher Mn but lower Al than γ phase, whereas κ-carbide shows the highest Mn and Al contents. In contrast, the 900 °C specimen exhibits a duplex α+γ microstructure lacking obvious matrix κ-carbide, and the line scan displays relatively uniform elemental distributions without sharp Mn/Al peaks. These results support that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide lies between 850 °C and 900 °C, with κ-carbide at α/γ boundaries dissolving before that along α/α grain boundaries.

The figure presents a line chart showing the evolution of phase volume fractions as a function of annealing temperature for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel, determined from XRD measurements. The x-axis spans from the hot-rolled (HR) state through annealing temperatures of 700 °C to 1200 °C, while the y-axis indicates phase volume fraction from 0 to 1.0. Three curves are plotted: α-ferrite (red squares), γ-austenite (blue triangles), and κ-carbide (orange stars). The α phase peaks at approximately 0.84 at 700 °C, then decreases sharply to about 0.40 at 900 °C, after which it remains relatively stable between 0.37 and 0.43 up to 1200 °C. The γ phase is absent at 700 °C, increases monotonically with temperature to reach roughly 0.60 at 900 °C, peaks near 0.63 at 1000 °C, and then gradually declines to about 0.57 at 1200 °C. The κ-carbide fraction decreases continuously from approximately 0.16 at 700 °C to near zero between 850 °C and 900 °C; the caption notes it cannot be detected by XRD at 900 °C, indicating complete dissolution. These trends confirm that κ-carbide dissolution occurs between 850 °C and 900 °C, higher than the 823 °C predicted by Thermo-Calc, and that the steel enters a stable α + γ duplex regime above 900 °C with only minor phase fraction variations, providing a microstructural basis for interpreting subsequent tensile property changes.

This figure presents tensile mechanical property curves for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at 700–1200°C for 1 h. Panel (a) shows engineering stress–engineering strain curves: the 700°C specimen exhibits discontinuous yielding with an upper yield point near 970 MPa, whereas specimens annealed at 800–1200°C display continuous yielding. As annealing temperature increases, the engineering stress curves systematically shift downward, indicating decreased strength, while elongation first increases from 700°C to 900°C and then decreases toward 1200°C. Panel (b) overlays true stress–true strain curves with work hardening rate curves. For specimens annealed at 700°C, 800°C, and 850°C, where κ-carbide is present in the matrix, the work hardening rates at true strain below ~5% are in reverse order of annealing temperature and drop rapidly until failure. In contrast, specimens annealed at 900°C and above, with no obvious matrix κ-carbide, show lower initial work hardening rates but sustain uniform deformation to larger strains. The 900°C specimen achieves the optimal combination with an ultimate tensile strength of 855 MPa and elongation of 36.6%, yielding the best strength–ductility product of 31 GPa%. These curves demonstrate that κ-carbide dissolution around 900°C eliminates yield point phenomena and enables superior work hardening capacity and ductility.

Figure 16 presents a multi-axis line graph showing the variation of tensile properties of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel as a function of annealing temperature (700–1200 °C) for 1 h, with the hot-rolled (HR) state included for comparison. The left y-axis reports strength in MPa, while the right y-axes display elongation to failure (%) and the product of strength and ductility (GPa%). Four data series are plotted: tensile strength (red squares), yield strength (magenta diamonds), ultimate elongation (green circles), and the product of strength and ductility (blue stars). Both tensile and yield strengths decrease monotonically with increasing annealing temperature, dropping from approximately 1100 MPa and 1000 MPa in the HR condition to about 611 MPa and 495 MPa at 1200 °C. In contrast, ultimate elongation initially rises from ~5% at 700 °C to a peak of ~36.6% at 900 °C, then declines sharply at higher temperatures. The product of strength and ductility follows a similar trend to elongation, reaching a maximum of approximately 31 GPa% at 900 °C. This figure supports the conclusion that annealing at 900 °C for 1 h provides the optimal balance of strength and ductility, which is closely related to κ-carbide dissolution and grain growth behavior.

This figure presents SEM fractographs of tensile fracture surfaces for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at 800 °C, 900 °C, and 1000 °C for 1 h. Subfigures (a), (c), and (e) are low-magnification images showing well-developed large secondary cracks in all specimens, which caption states are related to the banded microstructure. Subfigures (b), (d), and (f) provide high-magnification views revealing mixed fracture modes. At 800 °C, the fracture surface exhibits a mixture of ductile fracture (dimpled areas from γ phase) and quasi-cleavage fracture (river patterns from α phase), with κ-carbide along grain boundaries contributing to cleavage. At 900 °C, the ductile fracture area is more pronounced, correlating with enhanced ductility, whereas at 1000 °C the ductile region lessens and quasi-cleavage features become dominant. These observations support the core conclusion that annealing temperature governs tensile properties and fracture behavior by modulating the α/γ phase fraction and grain size.
